---
title: ""
---

::: {.content-section}

```{=html}
<div class="about-grid">
  <div class="about-photo">
    <!-- Replace div below with: <img src="assets/profile.jpg" alt="Dana C. Andersen"> -->
    <div class="photo-placeholder">Photo<br>coming<br>soon</div>
  </div>
  <div class="about-bio">
    <h1 style="font-family:'Lora',serif;font-size:1.9rem;font-weight:600;color:#1b2a3b;margin-bottom:0.2rem;">Dana C. Andersen</h1>
    <p style="color:#4a8fa8;font-size:1rem;font-weight:500;margin-bottom:1.2rem;">Associate Professor · Department of Economics · University of Alberta</p>
```

My research interests are in **environmental and resource economics**, **public economics**, and **sustainable development**. Much of my work examines how firms respond to environmental regulations, credit market conditions, and tax policy — and the consequences of these responses for environmental quality, productivity, and labor markets.

```{=html}
    <div class="contact-line">
      <a href="mailto:dca@ualberta.ca">dca@ualberta.ca</a>
      &nbsp;·&nbsp; 9-23 HM Tory Building, University of Alberta, Edmonton AB T6G 2H4
    </div>
  </div>
</div>
```

:::

::: {.content-section}

## Selected Publications {.section-heading}

```{=html}
<ul class="paper-list">

  <li class="paper-item">
    <div class="paper-title">Default Risk, Productivity, and the Environment: Theory and Evidence from U.S. Manufacturing</div>
    <div class="paper-meta"><em>Environmental and Resource Economics</em>, 2020, 75(4): 677–710</div>
  </li>

  <li class="paper-item">
    <div class="paper-title">Accounting for Loss of Variety and Factor Reallocations in the Welfare Cost of Regulations</div>
    <div class="paper-meta"><em>Journal of Environmental Economics and Management</em>, 2018, 88: 69–94</div>
  </li>

  <li class="paper-item">
    <div class="paper-title">Do Credit Constraints Favor Dirty Production? Theory and Plant-Level Evidence</div>
    <div class="paper-meta"><em>Journal of Environmental Economics and Management</em>, 2017, 84: 189–208</div>
  </li>

  <li class="paper-item">
    <div class="paper-title">Credit Constraints, Technology Upgrading, and the Environment</div>
    <div class="paper-meta"><em>Journal of the Association of Environmental and Resource Economists</em>, 2016, 3(2): 283–319</div>
  </li>

</ul>
<p style="margin-top:0.5rem;"><a href="research.html" style="color:#2d6a8a;font-size:0.9rem;">View all publications →</a></p>
```

:::
